title: The Research Process & the Research Onion
simple_html:
Business research is a systematic way of answering a question with evidence rather than opinion. Before you collect a single piece of data, you have to make a chain of connected decisions: what you believe counts as knowledge, how you will reason, what overall plan you will use, and finally the practical steps of gathering and analysing data. The Research Onion is a simple picture that organises all of these decisions into layers.

The layers, from outside in

  • Philosophy — your view of what reality and valid knowledge are (the outer skin).
  • Approach — whether you test a theory (deductive) or build one (inductive).
  • Strategy — the overall method, e.g. survey, experiment, case study.
  • Choices — using numbers (quantitative), words (qualitative), or both (mixed).
  • Time horizon — a snapshot (cross-sectional) or over time (longitudinal).
  • Techniques & procedures — the core: actually collecting and analysing data.

Why it matters

The big idea is coherence: each layer should fit the ones around it. If your inner choices contradict your outer beliefs, your study falls apart. Peeling the onion forces you to justify every decision in order.

Key takeaways

  • Research is a sequence of justified decisions, not just data gathering.
  • The onion has six layers, outer (beliefs) to inner (data techniques).
  • Layers must be consistent with each other.
  • Start every project at the outside and work inward.
  • A clear research question is the anchor that the whole onion serves.
intermediate_html:
The research process turns a vague curiosity into defensible knowledge. Saunders, Lewis and Thornhill's Research Onion is the standard MBA device for sequencing the decisions involved, because it shows that methods (the visible part) sit on top of deeper philosophical commitments that must be made first and made consistently.

The six layers in order

  1. Philosophy — assumptions about reality (ontology) and knowledge (epistemology): positivism, interpretivism, realism, pragmatism.
  2. Approach to theory development — deductive (start with theory, derive hypotheses, test), inductive (start with data, build theory), or abductive (move back and forth).
  3. Methodological choice — mono-method quantitative, mono-method qualitative, or mixed methods.
  4. Strategy — experiment, survey, case study, action research, grounded theory, ethnography, archival research.
  5. Time horizon — cross-sectional (one point in time) or longitudinal (repeated over time).
  6. Techniques & procedures — the core: sampling, data collection instruments, and analysis.

The golden thread

Examiners look for a golden thread: research question → objectives → design → data → analysis → conclusion, all logically linked. Every onion layer should serve the research question, and adjacent layers must be compatible. A positivist philosophy naturally pairs with a deductive approach, quantitative methods, a survey or experiment strategy, and statistical analysis. An interpretivist philosophy pairs with inductive reasoning, qualitative methods, and a case study or ethnography.

Worked mini-example

Suppose a manager asks, "Does flexible working increase employee productivity?" Peeling the onion:

  • Philosophy: positivist — productivity is measurable, the relationship is objective.
  • Approach: deductive — hypothesis: flexible working raises productivity.
  • Choice: quantitative.
  • Strategy: survey of staff plus archival output data, or a quasi-experiment comparing teams.
  • Time horizon: longitudinal — measure before and after a policy change.
  • Techniques: probability sample, validated questionnaire, regression analysis.

Notice how the inner choices were dictated by the outer ones. Had the question instead been "How do employees experience flexible working?", the word experience signals interpretivism, an inductive approach, qualitative interviews, and thematic analysis.

How to apply it

  1. Write a sharp, answerable research question and 2–4 objectives.
  2. Decide your philosophy honestly — let the question's verb (test, measure vs. explore, understand) guide you.
  3. Move inward, justifying each layer and checking it fits the one above.
  4. Document the choices; this becomes your methodology chapter.

This lesson sets up the rest of the course: Research Philosophy (Lesson 2), Approaches & Strategies (Lesson 3), and the practical layers covered in Lessons 4–8.
full_html:
The research process is the disciplined transformation of a question into credible, communicable knowledge. In business and management studies it is most commonly scaffolded by the Research Onion (Saunders, Lewis & Thornhill, 2019), a metaphor in which methodological decisions are arranged as concentric layers that the researcher peels from the outside in. The onion's pedagogical power is that it makes explicit a claim often left implicit: the visible methods of a study are the surface expression of deeper, prior philosophical commitments, and a study is only as sound as the coherence between these layers.

What 'business research' is

Research is distinguished from everyday problem-solving by being systematic (following a logical, transparent sequence), sceptical (claims are subjected to scrutiny), and ethical (Saunders et al., 2019). It produces knowledge that others can evaluate and, in principle, replicate or challenge. Business research is also typically applied as much as basic — oriented toward problems of practice, sitting on a continuum from pure theory development to consultancy (Easterby-Smith, Thorpe & Jackson, 2015).

The six layers

LayerQuestion it answersMain options
PhilosophyWhat is real and what counts as knowledge?Positivism, critical realism, interpretivism, postmodernism, pragmatism
ApproachHow is theory used?Deductive, inductive, abductive
Methodological choiceNumbers, words, or both?Mono-method, multi-method, mixed methods
StrategyWhat is the overall plan?Experiment, survey, case study, action research, grounded theory, ethnography, archival
Time horizonOne snapshot or over time?Cross-sectional, longitudinal
Techniques & proceduresHow is data gathered and analysed?Sampling, instruments, statistical or thematic analysis

Reasoning logics

Deduction, rooted in the hypothetico-deductive model (Popper, 1959), moves from a general theory to specific, falsifiable hypotheses that are tested against data; corroboration strengthens (but never proves) the theory, while a single disconfirming case can refute it. Induction reverses the direction: it builds theory up from observed patterns, accepting that conclusions are probabilistic rather than certain — the problem of induction (Hume) means no finite set of observations guarantees a universal law. Abduction (Peirce) seeks the best explanation for a surprising observation and iterates between data and theory; it underpins much pragmatic and mixed-methods work (Bryman & Bell, 2015).

The golden thread and the principle of alignment

The central discipline the onion enforces is alignment (sometimes called the "golden thread"): research question → objectives → philosophy → approach → design → data → analysis → conclusion must form an unbroken logical chain. Crotty (1998) frames the same idea as four linked elements — epistemology, theoretical perspective, methodology, and methods — each justifying the next. Misalignment is the most common and most damaging flaw in student dissertations: e.g., claiming an interpretivist stance but then running a closed-ended survey and reporting frequencies, or asserting a positivist stance while drawing strong causal claims from three unstructured interviews.

Worked example

Research question: "What is the effect of a four-day work week on team productivity in a software firm?"

  1. Philosophy: The verb effect and the measurable outcome signal positivism / critical realism.
  2. Approach: Deductive — H1: a four-day week increases output per FTE.
  3. Choice: Quantitative (possibly mixed, adding interviews to explain mechanisms).
  4. Strategy: Quasi-experiment — treatment teams move to four days, control teams remain on five.
  5. Time horizon: Longitudinal — baseline, 3-month, 6-month measures.
  6. Techniques: Stratified sample, validated productivity metrics, difference-in-differences regression with controls.

Each inner decision is constrained by the outer ones; swapping the question to "How do developers make sense of a compressed week?" would cascade changes through every layer toward interpretivism, induction, qualitative interviews, and thematic analysis.

Nuances and debates

First, the onion is a heuristic, not a rigid recipe; pragmatists (following Dewey) argue the research question, not a prior philosophical allegiance, should drive method selection, dissolving the so-called "paradigm wars" between positivism and interpretivism (Tashakkori & Teddlie, 1998). Second, critics note the onion can imply a tidy linearity that real research rarely follows — designs are often iterative and emergent, especially in grounded theory and action research. Third, the layers are not perfectly nested: time horizon and strategy interact, and some scholars prefer Crotty's (1998) four-level scheme as philosophically cleaner.

Common mistakes

  • Choosing a method first ("I'll do a survey") and back-filling the philosophy — reverses the logic of the onion.
  • Confusing the approach (deductive/inductive) with the choice (quantitative/qualitative); they correlate but are distinct.
  • Treating "mixed methods" as a way to avoid choosing, rather than a deliberate integration design.
  • Ignoring the time horizon, then making change-over-time claims from cross-sectional data.

Cross-links

The outer layer is unpacked in Research Philosophy (Lesson 2); the approach and strategy layers in Approaches & Research Strategies (Lesson 3); design and the literature review in Lesson 4; and the inner techniques across Sampling (Lesson 5), Data Collection & Measurement (Lesson 6), and Data Analysis (Lesson 8). Mastering the onion as a whole is what lets you defend each of those choices in a viva.

concepts.label: Nature of business research / research onion
